requirements_for_qualification.summary: Must be self-employed with no employees (except spouse) to establish Solo 401(k) enabling employee deferrals plus employer profit-sharing contributions up to $70,000 annually providing maximum retirement savings for solo entrepreneurs
criteria: Self-employed individual with no full-time employees other than spouse
2025 limits: $23,500 employee deferrals ($31,000 if age 50+) PLUS employer contribution up to 25% compensation
Total contribution limit: $70,000 (or $77,500 with age 50+ catch-up)
Calculation: Employee deferral up to $23,500/$31,000 + employer contribution of 20% net self-employment income
Roth option available for employee deferrals in many Solo 401(k) plans
Loan provisions permitted; can borrow up to $50,000 or 50% of account value
Must establish plan by December 31; contributions can be made until tax filing deadline with extensions
Form 5500-EZ required if plan assets exceed $250,000 at year-end

solo_401k_advantages.maximum_contributions: Employee deferral ($23,500-$31,000) + employer contribution (20% SE income) = up to $70,000+
solo_401k_advantages.higher_than_sep: Employee deferral component allows higher contributions than SEP-IRA at same income level
solo_401k_advantages.roth_option: Many plans offer Roth option for employee deferrals providing tax-free growth opportunity
solo_401k_advantages.loan_provision: Can borrow up to $50,000 or 50% of vested balance for personal needs
solo_401k_advantages.spousal_participation: Spouse can participate doubling household retirement savings

restrictions: Cannot have full-time employees other than owner and spouse (part-time under 1,000 hours generally okay)
More administrative complexity than SEP-IRA; must file Form 5500-EZ if assets exceed $250,000
Must establish plan by December 31 of year (contributions can be made by filing deadline)
Employer contribution limited to 20% of net self-employment income (after SE tax deduction)
Loan feature adds complexity; must follow specific repayment schedule or deemed distribution occurs
Prohibited transaction rules apply; cannot engage in self-dealing with plan assets
Must maintain separate plan for each unrelated business if multiple self-employment activities
RMDs required starting age 73 (75 if born 1960+) for pre-tax contributions and earnings
